title: Predicting the Brittle-to-Ductile Transition in Amorphous Polymers
authors: Valeriy V. Ginzburg, Oleg Gendelman, Alessio Zaccone
abstract: Brittle-ductile transition (BDT) is an important characteristic of amorphous (and semicrystalline) polymers. For a given strain rate, at temperatures above BDT, the polymers exhibit strain softening followed by yield and strain hardening, while at temperatures below BDT, the same materials exhibit brittle failure at relatively low strains. Surprisingly, today there is no simple model describing BDT as a function of polymer chemistry, sample history, deformation type, and strain rate. Experimental data suggest that BDT is often, though not always, associated with the beta-transition. We formulate a simple scalar model to describe the visco-elasto-plastic shear stress-strain curves as functions of temperature and strain rate. We also show that within this model, there is always an upper bound on the strain rate where the material can have a uniform viscoplastic flow; this upper bound is taken to represent the BDT. We stipulate that this upper bound is inversely proportional to the Johari-Goldstein beta-relaxation time. Using our "general" Sanchez-Lacombe "two-state, two-(time)scale" (SL-TS2) model, we compute the BDT for three polymers (polystyrene, poly(methylmethacrylate), and poly(vinylchloride)) and found a good agreement with experimental data.
summary: This paper proposes a simple scalar model to predict the brittle-to-ductile transition in amorphous polymers by identifying it as a stability limit of uniform flow. The model links the transition directly to the beta-relaxation timescale. It successfully reproduces experimental data for several common polymers.
main_problem: The lack of a simple, unified model to predict the Brittle-to-Ductile Transition (BDT) in amorphous polymers as a function of chemistry, strain rate, and temperature.
main_result: The authors identify the BDT as the upper bound on the strain rate for uniform viscoplastic flow, which is inversely proportional to the Johari-Goldstein beta-relaxation time.
method: A theoretical approach treating the BDT as a stability limit (similar to a spinodal line) using a scalar 1D visco-elasto-plastic model.
model_or_system: The system consists of amorphous polymers (PS, PMMA, PVC) modeled via the Sanchez-Lacombe 'two-state, two-time-scale' (SL-TS2) framework, treating the material as a dynamic coexistence of solid and liquid domains.
key_observables: Stress-strain curves, yield stress, brittle fracture stress, and BDT temperature.
important_parameters: Temperature (T), strain rate (S), glass transition temperature (Tg), alpha and beta relaxation times, and the efficiency parameter (x).
main_assumptions: The model is a 1D scalar approximation, assumes volume-preserving (incompressible) deformation, and neglects complex fracture mechanics like crack nucleation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows stress relaxation; Figure 2 shows the three mechanical response types; Figure 3 shows normalized elastic stress; Figure 4 shows the determinant function for phase boundaries; Figure 5 shows a phase diagram in the (B, Y) plane; Figures 6-8 compare model predictions to experimental BDT data for specific polymers.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the BDT problem, defines a 1D visco-elasto-plastic model based on SL-TS2 theory, derives a mathematical criterion for the loss of stability in flow, establishes a phase diagram for different mechanical regimes, and validates the model against experimental data for PS, PMMA, and PVC.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
